obj.title: Green roofs
obj.content:
[p:a]Rooftops covered with grass, vegetable gardens and lush foliage are now a common sight in many cities around the world. More and more private companies and city authorities are investing in green roofs, drawn to their wide-ranging benefits. Among the benefits are saving on energy costs, mitigating the risk of floods, making habitats for urban wildlife, tackling air pollution and even growing food. These increasingly radical urban designs can help cities adapt to the monumental problems they face, such as access to resources and a lack of green space due to development. But the involvement of city authorities, businesses and other institutions is crucial to ensuring their success – as is research investigating different options to suit the variety of rooftop spaces found in cities. The UK is relatively new to developing green roofs, and local governments and institutions are playing a major role in spreading the practice. London is home to much of the UK's green roof market, mainly due to forward-thinking policies such as the London Plan, which has paved the way to more than doubling the area of green roofs in the capital.[br/][br/]
[p:b]Ongoing research is showcasing how green roofs in cities can integrate with 'living walls': environmentally friendly walls which are partially or completely covered with greenery, including a growing medium, such as soil or water. Research also indicates that green roofs can be integrated with drainage systems on the ground, such as street trees, so that the water is managed better and the built environment is made more sustainable. There is also evidence to demonstrate the social value of green roofs. Doctors are increasingly prescribing time spent gardening outdoors for patients dealing with anxiety and depression. And research has found that access to even the most basic green spaces can provide a better quality of life for dementia sufferers and help people avoid obesity.[br/][br/]
[p:c]In North America, green roofs have become mainstream, with a wide array of expansive, accessible and food-producing roofs installed in buildings. Again, city leaders and authorities have helped push the movement forward – only recently, San Francisco, USA, created a policy requiring new buildings to have green roofs. Toronto, Canada, has policies dating from the 1990s, encouraging the development of urban farms on rooftops. These countries also benefit from having newer buildings than in many parts of the world, which makes it easier to install green roofs. Being able to keep enough water at roof height and distribute it right across the rooftop is crucial to maintaining the plants on any green roof – especially on 'edible roofs' where fruit and vegetables are farmed. And it's much easier to do this in newer buildings, which can typically hold greater weight, than to retro-fit old ones. Having a stronger roof also makes it easier to grow a greater variety of plants, since the soil can be deeper.[br/][br/]
[p:d]For green roofs to become the norm for new developments, there needs to be support from public authorities and private investors. Those responsible for maintaining buildings may have to acquire new skills, such as landscaping, and in some cases, volunteers may be needed to help out. Other considerations include installing drainage paths, meeting health and safety requirements and perhaps allowing access for the public, as well as planning restrictions and disruption from regular activities in and around the buildings during installation. To convince investors and developers that installing green roofs is worthwhile, economic arguments are still the most important. The term 'natural capital' has been developed to explain the economic value of nature; for example, measuring the money saved by installing natural solutions to protect against flood damage, adapt to climate change or help people lead healthier and happier lives.[br/][br/]
[p:e]As the expertise about green roofs grows, official standards have been developed to ensure that they are designed, constructed and maintained properly, and function well. Improvements in the science and technology underpinning green roof development have also led to new variations in the concept. For example, 'blue roofs' enable buildings to hold water over longer periods of time, rather than draining it away quickly – crucial in times of heavier rainfall. There are also combinations of green roofs with solar panels, and 'brown roofs' which are wilder in nature and maximise biodiversity. If the trend continues, it could create new jobs and a more vibrant and sustainable local food economy – alongside many other benefits. There are still barriers to overcome, but the evidence so far indicates that green roofs have the potential to transform cities and help them function sustainably long into the future. The success stories need to be studied and replicated elsewhere, to make green, blue, brown and food-producing roofs the norm in cities around the world.
obj.content_zh:
[b]A.[/b]在世界各地的许多城市，屋顶上铺满青草、菜园和郁郁葱葱的树叶已成为一道常见的风景。越来越多的私营公司和城市当局投资于屋顶绿化，因为屋顶绿化具有广泛的好处。这些好处包括节约能源成本、降低洪水风险、为城市野生动物提供栖息地、解决空气污染问题，甚至还能种植粮食。这些日益激进的城市设计可以帮助城市适应其面临的巨大问题，如资源获取和因开发而导致的绿地缺乏。但是，城市当局、企业和其他机构的参与对于确保其成功至关重要--研究不同的方案以适应城市屋顶空间的多样性也是如此。英国在发展屋顶绿化方面相对较新，地方政府和机构在推广屋顶绿化方面发挥着重要作用。伦敦是英国大部分屋顶绿化市场的发源地，这主要归功于伦敦计划等具有前瞻性的政策，该计划为将首都屋顶绿化面积增加一倍以上铺平了道路。[br/]
[b]B.[/b]正在进行的研究展示了城市中的绿色屋顶如何与 “活墙 ”相结合：即部分或全部用绿色植物（包括土壤或水等生长介质）覆盖的环保型墙壁。研究还表明，屋顶绿化可以与地面排水系统（如行道树）相结合，从而更好地管理水资源，使建筑环境更具可持续性。还有证据表明屋顶绿化的社会价值。越来越多的医生为焦虑症和抑郁症患者开出户外园艺的处方。研究发现，即使是最基本的绿地，也能提高痴呆症患者的生活质量，帮助人们避免肥胖。[br/]
[b]C.[/b]在北美洲，屋顶绿化已成为主流，在建筑物中安装了大量宽阔、方便和可生产食物的屋顶。同样，城市领导者和管理机构也帮助推动了这一运动的发展--就在最近，美国旧金山制定了一项政策，要求新建筑必须有绿色屋顶。加拿大多伦多早在 20 世纪 90 年代就制定了政策，鼓励在屋顶发展城市农场。与世界上许多地方相比，这些国家的建筑较新，因此更容易安装屋顶绿化设施。能够在屋顶高度保持足够的水量，并将其直接分配到整个屋顶，对于维护任何绿色屋顶上的植物都至关重要，尤其是在种植水果和蔬菜的 “可食用屋顶 ”上。新建筑通常能承受更大的重量，因此在新建筑中做到这一点要比改造旧建筑容易得多。屋顶更坚固也更容易种植更多种类的植物，因为土壤可以更深。[br/]
[b]D.[/b]要使屋顶绿化成为新开发项目的规范，需要得到公共机构和私人投资者的支持。负责维护建筑物的人员可能需要学习新的技能，如景观美化，在某些情况下，可能需要志愿者提供帮助。其他考虑因素还包括安装排水路径、满足健康和安全要求、可能允许公众进入，以及规划限制和安装期间对建筑物内和周围正常活动的干扰。要说服投资者和开发商安装绿色屋顶是值得的，经济方面的论据仍然是最重要的。自然资本 "一词被用来解释大自然的经济价值；例如，衡量通过安装自然解决方案来防止洪水破坏、适应气候变化或帮助人们过上更健康、更快乐的生活所节省的资金。[br/]
[b]E.[/b]随着绿色屋顶专业知识的增长，已经制定了官方标准，以确保其设计、建造和维护得当，并发挥良好的功能。作为绿色屋顶发展基础的科学技术的进步也使这一概念有了新的变化。例如，“蓝色屋顶 ”使建筑物能够长时间蓄水，而不是快速排水--这在降雨量较大时至关重要。此外，还有绿色屋顶与太阳能电池板的组合，以及 “棕色屋顶”，后者更具有野性，能最大限度地提高生物多样性。如果这一趋势持续下去，它将创造新的就业机会，并为当地食品经济带来更多活力和可持续发展，同时还能带来许多其他好处。虽然仍有一些障碍需要克服，但迄今为止的证据表明，绿色屋顶有可能改变城市，帮助城市在未来长期可持续地运转。我们需要对成功案例进行研究，并将其推广到其他地方，使绿色、蓝色、棕色和可生产粮食的屋顶成为世界各地城市的常态。

More and more private companies and city authorities are investing in green roofs, drawn to their wide-ranging benefits. Among the benefits are saving on energy costs, mitigating the risk of floods, making habitats for urban wildlife, tackling air pollution and even growing food. There is also evidence to demonstrate the social value of green roofs. Doctors are increasingly prescribing time spent gardening outdoors for patients dealing with anxiety and depression. dFor green roofs to become the norm for new developments, there needs to be support from public authorities and private investors. There are also combinations of green roofs with solar panels, and 'brown roofs' which are wilder in nature and maximise biodiversity. If the trend continues, it could create new jobs and a more vibrant and sustainable local food economy – alongside many other benefits. There are still barriers to overcome, but the evidence so far indicates that green roofs have the potential to transform cities and help them function sustainably long into the future. The success stories need to be studied and replicated elsewhere, to make green, blue, brown and food-producing roofs the norm in cities around the world.

obj.content:
[center][b]Advantages of green roofs[/b][/center][br/]
City rooftops covered with greenery have many advantages. These include lessening the likelihood that floods will occur, reducing how much money is spent on [blank]6[/blank] and creating environments that are suitable for wildlife. In many cases, they can also be used for producing [blank]7[/blank].[br/]

    There are also social benefits of green roofs. For example, the medical profession recommends [blank]8[/blank] as an activity to help people cope with mental health issues. Studies have also shown that the availability of green spaces can prevent physical problems such as [blank]9[/blank].
obj.html_content:
Advantages of green roofs


City rooftops covered with greenery have many advantages. These include lessening the likelihood that floods will occur, reducing how much money is spent on and creating environments that are suitable for wildlife. In many cases, they can also be used for producing  .
There are also social benefits of green roofs. For example, the medical profession recommends  as an activity to help people cope with mental health issues. Studies have also shown that the availability of green spaces can prevent physical problems such as  .

obj.html_content:
aRooftops covered with grass, vegetable gardens and lush foliage are now a common sight in many cities around the world. More and more private companies and city authorities are investing in green roofs, drawn to their wide-ranging benefits. Among the benefits are saving on energy costs, mitigating the risk of floods, making habitats for urban wildlife, tackling air pollution and even growing food. These increasingly radical urban designs can help cities adapt to the monumental problems they face, such as access to resources and a lack of green space due to development. But the involvement of city authorities, businesses and other institutions is crucial to ensuring their success – as is research investigating different options to suit the variety of rooftop spaces found in cities. The UK is relatively new to developing green roofs, and local governments and institutions are playing a major role in spreading the practice. London is home to much of the UK's green roof market, mainly due to forward-thinking policies such as the London Plan, which has paved the way to more than doubling the area of green roofs in the capital.



bOngoing research is showcasing how green roofs in cities can integrate with 'living walls': environmentally friendly walls which are partially or completely covered with greenery, including a growing medium, such as soil or water. Research also indicates that green roofs can be integrated with drainage systems on the ground, such as street trees, so that the water is managed better and the built environment is made more sustainable. There is also evidence to demonstrate the social value of green roofs. Doctors are increasingly prescribing time spent gardening outdoors for patients dealing with anxiety and depression. And research has found that access to even the most basic green spaces can provide a better quality of life for dementia sufferers and help people avoid obesity.



cIn North America, green roofs have become mainstream, with a wide array of expansive, accessible and food-producing roofs installed in buildings. Again, city leaders and authorities have helped push the movement forward – only recently, San Francisco, USA, created a policy requiring new buildings to have green roofs. Toronto, Canada, has policies dating from the 1990s, encouraging the development of urban farms on rooftops. These countries also benefit from having newer buildings than in many parts of the world, which makes it easier to install green roofs. Being able to keep enough water at roof height and distribute it right across the rooftop is crucial to maintaining the plants on any green roof – especially on 'edible roofs' where fruit and vegetables are farmed. And it's much easier to do this in newer buildings, which can typically hold greater weight, than to retro-fit old ones. Having a stronger roof also makes it easier to grow a greater variety of plants, since the soil can be deeper.



dFor green roofs to become the norm for new developments, there needs to be support from public authorities and private investors. Those responsible for maintaining buildings may have to acquire new skills, such as landscaping, and in some cases, volunteers may be needed to help out. Other considerations include installing drainage paths, meeting health and safety requirements and perhaps allowing access for the public, as well as planning restrictions and disruption from regular activities in and around the buildings during installation. To convince investors and developers that installing green roofs is worthwhile, economic arguments are still the most important. The term 'natural capital' has been developed to explain the economic value of nature; for example, measuring the money saved by installing natural solutions to protect against flood damage, adapt to climate change or help people lead healthier and happier lives.



eAs the expertise about green roofs grows, official standards have been developed to ensure that they are designed, constructed and maintained properly, and function well. Improvements in the science and technology underpinning green roof development have also led to new variations in the concept. For example, 'blue roofs' enable buildings to hold water over longer periods of time, rather than draining it away quickly – crucial in times of heavier rainfall. There are also combinations of green roofs with solar panels, and 'brown roofs' which are wilder in nature and maximise biodiversity. If the trend continues, it could create new jobs and a more vibrant and sustainable local food economy – alongside many other benefits. There are still barriers to overcome, but the evidence so far indicates that green roofs have the potential to transform cities and help them function sustainably long into the future. The success stories need to be studied and replicated elsewhere, to make green, blue, brown and food-producing roofs the norm in cities around the world.

obj.title: Green roofs
obj.content:
[p:a]Rooftops covered with grass, vegetable gardens and lush foliage are now a common sight in many cities around the world. More and more private companies and city authorities are investing in green roofs, drawn to their wide-ranging benefits. Among the benefits are saving on energy costs, mitigating the risk of floods, making habitats for urban wildlife, tackling air pollution and even growing food. These increasingly radical urban designs can help cities adapt to the monumental problems they face, such as access to resources and a lack of green space due to development. But the involvement of city authorities, businesses and other institutions is crucial to ensuring their success – as is research investigating different options to suit the variety of rooftop spaces found in cities. The UK is relatively new to developing green roofs, and local governments and institutions are playing a major role in spreading the practice. London is home to much of the UK's green roof market, mainly due to forward-thinking policies such as the London Plan, which has paved the way to more than doubling the area of green roofs in the capital.[br/][br/]
[p:b]Ongoing research is showcasing how green roofs in cities can integrate with 'living walls': environmentally friendly walls which are partially or completely covered with greenery, including a growing medium, such as soil or water. Research also indicates that green roofs can be integrated with drainage systems on the ground, such as street trees, so that the water is managed better and the built environment is made more sustainable. There is also evidence to demonstrate the social value of green roofs. Doctors are increasingly prescribing time spent gardening outdoors for patients dealing with anxiety and depression. And research has found that access to even the most basic green spaces can provide a better quality of life for dementia sufferers and help people avoid obesity.[br/][br/]
[p:c]In North America, green roofs have become mainstream, with a wide array of expansive, accessible and food-producing roofs installed in buildings. Again, city leaders and authorities have helped push the movement forward – only recently, San Francisco, USA, created a policy requiring new buildings to have green roofs. Toronto, Canada, has policies dating from the 1990s, encouraging the development of urban farms on rooftops. These countries also benefit from having newer buildings than in many parts of the world, which makes it easier to install green roofs. Being able to keep enough water at roof height and distribute it right across the rooftop is crucial to maintaining the plants on any green roof – especially on 'edible roofs' where fruit and vegetables are farmed. And it's much easier to do this in newer buildings, which can typically hold greater weight, than to retro-fit old ones. Having a stronger roof also makes it easier to grow a greater variety of plants, since the soil can be deeper.[br/][br/]
[p:d]For green roofs to become the norm for new developments, there needs to be support from public authorities and private investors. Those responsible for maintaining buildings may have to acquire new skills, such as landscaping, and in some cases, volunteers may be needed to help out. Other considerations include installing drainage paths, meeting health and safety requirements and perhaps allowing access for the public, as well as planning restrictions and disruption from regular activities in and around the buildings during installation. To convince investors and developers that installing green roofs is worthwhile, economic arguments are still the most important. The term 'natural capital' has been developed to explain the economic value of nature; for example, measuring the money saved by installing natural solutions to protect against flood damage, adapt to climate change or help people lead healthier and happier lives.[br/][br/]
[p:e]As the expertise about green roofs grows, official standards have been developed to ensure that they are designed, constructed and maintained properly, and function well. Improvements in the science and technology underpinning green roof development have also led to new variations in the concept. For example, 'blue roofs' enable buildings to hold water over longer periods of time, rather than draining it away quickly – crucial in times of heavier rainfall. There are also combinations of green roofs with solar panels, and 'brown roofs' which are wilder in nature and maximise biodiversity. If the trend continues, it could create new jobs and a more vibrant and sustainable local food economy – alongside many other benefits. There are still barriers to overcome, but the evidence so far indicates that green roofs have the potential to transform cities and help them function sustainably long into the future. The success stories need to be studied and replicated elsewhere, to make green, blue, brown and food-producing roofs the norm in cities around the world.
obj.content_zh:
[b]A.[/b]在世界各地的许多城市，屋顶上铺满青草、菜园和郁郁葱葱的树叶已成为一道常见的风景。越来越多的私营公司和城市当局投资于屋顶绿化，因为屋顶绿化具有广泛的好处。这些好处包括节约能源成本、降低洪水风险、为城市野生动物提供栖息地、解决空气污染问题，甚至还能种植粮食。这些日益激进的城市设计可以帮助城市适应其面临的巨大问题，如资源获取和因开发而导致的绿地缺乏。但是，城市当局、企业和其他机构的参与对于确保其成功至关重要--研究不同的方案以适应城市屋顶空间的多样性也是如此。英国在发展屋顶绿化方面相对较新，地方政府和机构在推广屋顶绿化方面发挥着重要作用。伦敦是英国大部分屋顶绿化市场的发源地，这主要归功于伦敦计划等具有前瞻性的政策，该计划为将首都屋顶绿化面积增加一倍以上铺平了道路。[br/]
[b]B.[/b]正在进行的研究展示了城市中的绿色屋顶如何与 “活墙 ”相结合：即部分或全部用绿色植物（包括土壤或水等生长介质）覆盖的环保型墙壁。研究还表明，屋顶绿化可以与地面排水系统（如行道树）相结合，从而更好地管理水资源，使建筑环境更具可持续性。还有证据表明屋顶绿化的社会价值。越来越多的医生为焦虑症和抑郁症患者开出户外园艺的处方。研究发现，即使是最基本的绿地，也能提高痴呆症患者的生活质量，帮助人们避免肥胖。[br/]
[b]C.[/b]在北美洲，屋顶绿化已成为主流，在建筑物中安装了大量宽阔、方便和可生产食物的屋顶。同样，城市领导者和管理机构也帮助推动了这一运动的发展--就在最近，美国旧金山制定了一项政策，要求新建筑必须有绿色屋顶。加拿大多伦多早在 20 世纪 90 年代就制定了政策，鼓励在屋顶发展城市农场。与世界上许多地方相比，这些国家的建筑较新，因此更容易安装屋顶绿化设施。能够在屋顶高度保持足够的水量，并将其直接分配到整个屋顶，对于维护任何绿色屋顶上的植物都至关重要，尤其是在种植水果和蔬菜的 “可食用屋顶 ”上。新建筑通常能承受更大的重量，因此在新建筑中做到这一点要比改造旧建筑容易得多。屋顶更坚固也更容易种植更多种类的植物，因为土壤可以更深。[br/]
[b]D.[/b]要使屋顶绿化成为新开发项目的规范，需要得到公共机构和私人投资者的支持。负责维护建筑物的人员可能需要学习新的技能，如景观美化，在某些情况下，可能需要志愿者提供帮助。其他考虑因素还包括安装排水路径、满足健康和安全要求、可能允许公众进入，以及规划限制和安装期间对建筑物内和周围正常活动的干扰。要说服投资者和开发商安装绿色屋顶是值得的，经济方面的论据仍然是最重要的。自然资本 "一词被用来解释大自然的经济价值；例如，衡量通过安装自然解决方案来防止洪水破坏、适应气候变化或帮助人们过上更健康、更快乐的生活所节省的资金。[br/]
[b]E.[/b]随着绿色屋顶专业知识的增长，已经制定了官方标准，以确保其设计、建造和维护得当，并发挥良好的功能。作为绿色屋顶发展基础的科学技术的进步也使这一概念有了新的变化。例如，“蓝色屋顶 ”使建筑物能够长时间蓄水，而不是快速排水--这在降雨量较大时至关重要。此外，还有绿色屋顶与太阳能电池板的组合，以及 “棕色屋顶”，后者更具有野性，能最大限度地提高生物多样性。如果这一趋势持续下去，它将创造新的就业机会，并为当地食品经济带来更多活力和可持续发展，同时还能带来许多其他好处。虽然仍有一些障碍需要克服，但迄今为止的证据表明，绿色屋顶有可能改变城市，帮助城市在未来长期可持续地运转。我们需要对成功案例进行研究，并将其推广到其他地方，使绿色、蓝色、棕色和可生产粮食的屋顶成为世界各地城市的常态。

[b]C.[/b]在北美洲，屋顶绿化已成为主流，在建筑物中安装了大量宽阔、方便和可生产食物的屋顶。同样，城市领导者和管理机构也帮助推动了这一运动的发展--就在最近，美国旧金山制定了一项政策，要求新建筑必须有绿色屋顶。加拿大多伦多早在 20 世纪 90 年代就制定了政策，鼓励在屋顶发展城市农场。与世界上许多地方相比，这些国家的建筑较新，因此更容易安装屋顶绿化设施。能够在屋顶高度保持足够的水量，并将其直接分配到整个屋顶，对于维护任何绿色屋顶上的植物都至关重要，尤其是在种植水果和蔬菜的 “可食用屋顶 ”上。新建筑通常能承受更大的重量，因此在新建筑中做到这一点要比改造旧建筑容易得多。屋顶更坚固也更容易种植更多种类的植物，因为土壤可以更深。[br/]
[b]D.[/b]要使屋顶绿化成为新开发项目的规范，需要得到公共机构和私人投资者的支持。负责维护建筑物的人员可能需要学习新的技能，如景观美化，在某些情况下，可能需要志愿者提供帮助。其他考虑因素还包括安装排水路径、满足健康和安全要求、可能允许公众进入，以及规划限制和安装期间对建筑物内和周围正常活动的干扰。要说服投资者和开发商安装绿色屋顶是值得的，经济方面的论据仍然是最重要的。自然资本 "一词被用来解释大自然的经济价值；例如，衡量通过安装自然解决方案来防止洪水破坏、适应气候变化或帮助人们过上更健康、更快乐的生活所节省的资金。[br/]
[b]E.[/b]随着绿色屋顶专业知识的增长，已经制定了官方标准，以确保其设计、建造和维护得当，并发挥良好的功能。作为绿色屋顶发展基础的科学技术的进步也使这一概念有了新的变化。例如，“蓝色屋顶 ”使建筑物能够长时间蓄水，而不是快速排水--这在降雨量较大时至关重要。此外，还有绿色屋顶与太阳能电池板的组合，以及 “棕色屋顶”，后者更具有野性，能最大限度地提高生物多样性。如果这一趋势持续下去，它将创造新的就业机会，并为当地食品经济带来更多活力和可持续发展，同时还能带来许多其他好处。虽然仍有一些障碍需要克服，但迄今为止的证据表明，绿色屋顶有可能改变城市，帮助城市在未来长期可持续地运转。我们需要对成功案例进行研究，并将其推广到其他地方，使绿色、蓝色、棕色和可生产粮食的屋顶成为世界各地城市的常态。
obj.html_content:
aRooftops covered with grass, vegetable gardens and lush foliage are now a common sight in many cities around the world. More and more private companies and city authorities are investing in green roofs, drawn to their wide-ranging benefits. Among the benefits are saving on energy costs, mitigating the risk of floods, making habitats for urban wildlife, tackling air pollution and even growing food. These increasingly radical urban designs can help cities adapt to the monumental problems they face, such as access to resources and a lack of green space due to development. But the involvement of city authorities, businesses and other institutions is crucial to ensuring their success – as is research investigating different options to suit the variety of rooftop spaces found in cities. The UK is relatively new to developing green roofs, and local governments and institutions are playing a major role in spreading the practice. London is home to much of the UK's green roof market, mainly due to forward-thinking policies such as the London Plan, which has paved the way to more than doubling the area of green roofs in the capital.



bOngoing research is showcasing how green roofs in cities can integrate with 'living walls': environmentally friendly walls which are partially or completely covered with greenery, including a growing medium, such as soil or water. Research also indicates that green roofs can be integrated with drainage systems on the ground, such as street trees, so that the water is managed better and the built environment is made more sustainable. There is also evidence to demonstrate the social value of green roofs. Doctors are increasingly prescribing time spent gardening outdoors for patients dealing with anxiety and depression. And research has found that access to even the most basic green spaces can provide a better quality of life for dementia sufferers and help people avoid obesity.



cIn North America, green roofs have become mainstream, with a wide array of expansive, accessible and food-producing roofs installed in buildings. Again, city leaders and authorities have helped push the movement forward – only recently, San Francisco, USA, created a policy requiring new buildings to have green roofs. Toronto, Canada, has policies dating from the 1990s, encouraging the development of urban farms on rooftops. These countries also benefit from having newer buildings than in many parts of the world, which makes it easier to install green roofs. Being able to keep enough water at roof height and distribute it right across the rooftop is crucial to maintaining the plants on any green roof – especially on 'edible roofs' where fruit and vegetables are farmed. And it's much easier to do this in newer buildings, which can typically hold greater weight, than to retro-fit old ones. Having a stronger roof also makes it easier to grow a greater variety of plants, since the soil can be deeper.



dFor green roofs to become the norm for new developments, there needs to be support from public authorities and private investors. Those responsible for maintaining buildings may have to acquire new skills, such as landscaping, and in some cases, volunteers may be needed to help out. Other considerations include installing drainage paths, meeting health and safety requirements and perhaps allowing access for the public, as well as planning restrictions and disruption from regular activities in and around the buildings during installation. To convince investors and developers that installing green roofs is worthwhile, economic arguments are still the most important. The term 'natural capital' has been developed to explain the economic value of nature; for example, measuring the money saved by installing natural solutions to protect against flood damage, adapt to climate change or help people lead healthier and happier lives.



eAs the expertise about green roofs grows, official standards have been developed to ensure that they are designed, constructed and maintained properly, and function well. Improvements in the science and technology underpinning green roof development have also led to new variations in the concept. For example, 'blue roofs' enable buildings to hold water over longer periods of time, rather than draining it away quickly – crucial in times of heavier rainfall. There are also combinations of green roofs with solar panels, and 'brown roofs' which are wilder in nature and maximise biodiversity. If the trend continues, it could create new jobs and a more vibrant and sustainable local food economy – alongside many other benefits. There are still barriers to overcome, but the evidence so far indicates that green roofs have the potential to transform cities and help them function sustainably long into the future. The success stories need to be studied and replicated elsewhere, to make green, blue, brown and food-producing roofs the norm in cities around the world.

officialExplain.explain: 本题的解答难度主要来自于对应原文的阅读量较大。题干的句意表达，由于并不包含困难词汇、句式结构也不复杂，因此并不难看懂。然而到了对应的D段里，考生却需要连看三句话，相当于看完了整个D段的一半篇幅还多，才能更有把握。先是第一句说要推广绿色屋顶需要官方和大众支持，再是第二句说目前那些从事建筑和装修工作的人需要学习新技能，可能有时还得找志愿者参与帮忙，接下来第三句又说还有其他这样那样的因素需要考虑。能耐心读完这三句，考生也就不难看出这些内容相当于都是在列举题干中所说的“需要克服的挑战”，至此才能笃定确认本题的答案是D。

本题题干中其实有一个隐藏的定位提示：此处的模糊概念a city，回到原文中将很有可能转化为一个具体的城市名称；而任何一个城市名称，都有着首字母大写这样的属性。于是，考生在阅读文章时可以优先寻找包含城市名大写信息的句子，大致定位后再更精细地阅读对比，从而提升解题效率。待在C段中找到了两个城市名扎堆出现的地方之后，解题动作其实也就完成了一大半。对于美国城市旧金山，句子里有明确的描述“only recently”，不符合题干要求；而加拿大城市多伦多推广绿色屋顶的做法则可以追溯到之前的20世纪90年代，基本对应了题干要求的“许多年”，可以帮助考生笃定地得出本题的正确答案为C。

本题的解答难度可能主要来自于对应原文的位置。考生需要一路看到文章最后一段的最后一句，才能找到相应的信息；且由于这种同义替换方式并非采用“一个词对应替换另一个词”的简单模式，因此还是需要考生耐心看懂全句，明白原文所说的“成功的故事应当被研究以后在其他地方模仿复制，令全球各处都遍布这类更环保的屋顶”，就是在用句意替换表达题干所说的“建造新建筑时我们应该以现在这些绿色屋顶为参照榜样”。若是想单独找某个词的同义替换，诚然是找不到的；但由于这两个句子都没有包含什么生词难词，句意理解难度也都不高，因此考生应当不难在耐心看懂之后得出正确答案为E。
本题其实主要考查考生对integrate这个雅思阅读高频词的掌握程度（目前还不认识这个词的同学请移步前文“必背词汇”，就地牢固掌握其词义）。B段的第一句话就使用了这个词来表示绿色屋顶可以与“生命墙”结合使用；接下来的第二句话再次使用了这个词来表示绿色屋顶还可以与排水系统结合在一起使用。很显然，这两个结合的对象就是题干中所说的“其他绿色城市举措”，因此考生当然也就不难得出本题答案为B。
本题除了位置靠后、全句平平无奇并不显眼之外，也就没什么解答难点了。无论是题干中的persuasive与原文句中对应的convince，还是题干中的financial与原文句中对应的economic，都是司空见惯的雅思阅读高频词。考生耐心看到相应位置和看懂意思后，就能非常确定地得出本题答案为D。

本题开篇就使用了指代词these，直白地告诉考生“还是要老老实实充分阅读题干为好”，否则就算刚才跳过了没有挖空的第一句话，现在看到these也还是得回去查看到底these指的是什么。好在第一句不长也不难，原来these指的是“屋顶一片绿”的诸般好处。题干中的advantages一词，很快就能在A段第三句话里找到对应的同义替换词benefits，于是考生只需耐心阅读对比，便可轻松在这句话所列举的众多好处中看到“节约能源花销”这个信息，进而锁定答案词应为energy。
本题与上一道题来自同一个原文句，这一点其实题干已经做了充分提示：无论是指代词they（这回指的是绿色屋顶）还是also，都在告诉考生此题空格内所需的答案还是与绿色屋顶的诸多好处有关。因此，足够耐心和细心的考生不难在对比之后，看出题干中的producing对应了原文中的growing，要生产的是食物，从而顺利得出答案应为food。

本题与前两道题比起来略难一点点，主要是因为题干和原文中所用词汇的认知难度比前两题略高。题干中的medical profession对应原文中的doctors；题干中的recommends对应原文中的prescribe；题干中的上义词词组mental health issues则对应原文中的两个具体下义词anxiety和depression。二者都是在说“医生会告诉有焦虑和抑郁类精神问题的病人进行园艺活动来改善自己的情况”。但要因此说本题不好做，却也不够公正，因为所有这些单词都是雅思阅读文章中的常客，虽非最基础的起步类词汇，但认知难度也绝对说不上很高。看懂了这些对应内容，考生也就应该锁定了正确答案应为gardening。

本题才算真正从某种程度上考查了难度词汇。阅读题干后不难看出：这里还是在讲绿色空间的好处，因此本题距离上一题必定不远；阅读原文内容后也不难看出：确实在接下来的一句里给出了相关信息。但是，B段最后一句话提到了两种不同的好处，一种是为dementia患者提供更好的生活质量，另一种是帮助人们避免obesity。如果考生词汇过关、认识dementia指的是“痴呆”或“精神错乱”这类精神类疾病，则可以通过题干中要求的是“身体类疾病”这个线索排除此项，得出结论。
不过，即使并不认识这个难度较高的词汇，考生也不必慌张，因为很多时候题干会给出不止一条答题线索，并非一味考查考生的单词量积累情况，而是更要求考生全面关注句子的整体意思。在这里，题干要求找到的是帮人们prevent身体问题，因此空格中所需的必然是某种不好的东西，对比原文会发现这个不认识的dementia患者其实是得到了好的东西，也就是better quality of life；反之要avoid的对象则是不好的obesity。从这个褒贬感情色彩出发，对比题干和进行排除，也可以确认正确答案为obesity。

选项A：a longer growing season for edible produce（可食用农产品的一个更长的生长期）如果将这条选项信息类比于雅思阅读中的另一个题型“判断题”，那么这句话就是一个典型的在原文中并未出现的NOT GIVEN题目。因为读遍了整个C段，也找不到对于这条信息的相关表述。读者在此倒是可以特别注意一下produce在此处的用法：指的是名词形式的“农产品”而非动词形式的“生产”。这个用法在雅思真题中被考查过不止一次，而且阅读和听力中都出现过它的身影，值得考生牢固掌握。

选项B：more economical use of water（更能节约用水）C段里确实提到了water这个信息，然而仔细对比原文意思不难看出，新建筑与水有关的属性是有更强的蓄水能力，而非更节约地使用水源，因此本项的干扰作用并不强。

选项C：greater water-storage capacity（更大的蓄水能力）这个选项直接对应了C段的倒数第三句话：“要能在屋顶高度储蓄足够的水并输送到屋顶各处去”，因此本项正是正确答案之一。对应原文句的语法结构略复杂，详细分析可以参见前文“佳句赏析”中的第四句。

选项D：ability to cultivate more plant types（可以种植更多的作物品种）这个选项连同其对应的C段最后一句话，都简短易懂，原文句用来表述“更容易种植更多植物品类”的greater variety与选项中的more plant types，更是连词汇难度都算不上高。考生应当可以顺利选中此项作为第二个正确答案。

选项E：a large surface area for growing plants（有更大的表面区域来种植作物）本项与A项类似，也是一条完全未在C段任何地方被提及过的、子虚乌有的内容。

本题的两个正确选项出处非常集中，来自原文同一句话；对考生词汇量的考查力度也算中等，像habitat、species、biodiversity、solar panel、generate power这些，虽不是最基础的词汇，但也绝对算不上生僻晦涩。考生只需拿出应有的耐心和细心来在E段中阅读寻找，应当就可以顺利解题。

选项A：to provide habitats for a wide range of species（为许多物种提供栖息地）原文对应句为考生示范了如何精简地用两个单词来同义替换选项中从provide开始的一共8个词的内容：maximise biodiversity。所谓“生物多样性”，就是“数量尽可能众多的物种”；所谓“最大化”，就是“为它们提供生存条件以利于其繁衍生息”。两相对比，本项正是符合题干要求的正确答案之一。 

选项B：to grow plants successfully even in the wettest climates（在即使最潮湿的气候里也能成功种植作物）这个选项里虽然提到了“潮湿”，也就是间接地与E段原文中说到的water和rainfall有某些关联，然而完整意思却与文中所说的“能在下大雨时蓄水更长时间而不是很快排空”毫无关系，因此干扰作用并不强。

选项C：to regulate the temperature of the immediate environment（调节周遭环境的温度）本项属于凭空捏造的一条信息，很好排除。不过读者倒是可以特别注意一下此处immediate这个词的用法，不是时间上的“立刻，马上”，而是空间上的“紧邻”。

选项D：to generate power from a sustainable source（利用一种可持续发展的源头来发电）这个正确选项对应的是原文句中的solar panels。如前所说，只要考生的核心考点词汇认知能力达标，知道“太阳能发电板”的作用就是利用“太阳能”这种可持续资源来进行“发电”，就能准确锁定本项为第二个正确答案。

选项E：to collect water to supply other buildings（收集水以供应其他建筑）这个选项与前面的B项有异曲同工之处，都属于虽然包含了water这个词、但总体意思却与原文表述风马牛不相及的情况，因此应该不会干扰考生作答。
